payload.answer: Store the dataset as a structured artifact: persist the table schema, row count, repetition/normalization metadata, and a bounded sample or deduplicated value sets, plus an index key (e.g., id) and a reference to the raw source for exact retrieval when needed.
payload.decision: Do not store the full 500-row CSV contents as-is; store a summarized/structured representation (schema + sampling + indexing metadata) and keep the source reference instead, due to size and repetition.

entity_resolution.reason: Entities are generic (dataset/columns) and do not require external identifier mapping.

payload.intent: Determine how to store the provided large CSV table in the Brain personal memory system.

relationships.object: CSV table with ~500 rows and repeated id/name/value
relationships.subject: large_table_500_rows_001

memory_cards.statement: For a large CSV table (~500 rows) with repeated id/name/value patterns, prefer storing a structured summary (schema, row count, and/or representative samples) plus indexing metadata rather than the full raw contents.

When the dataset is repetitive, store normalization metadata (e.g., column meanings, deduplicated distinct values if applicable) and an access mechanism (e.g., retrieve by id) instead of duplicating repeated rows.

entities.name: retrieval requirements
entities.role: determines whether raw fidelity is required

relationships.object: whether to store raw row contents
relationships.subject: retrieval requirements

memory_cards.statement: The optimal storage strategy depends on the intended retrieval queries (e.g., whether exact row fidelity is required). If exact row-level answers are needed, store full rows or a compressed/append-only raw reference with an index.

details: Extracted storage-policy guidance under ambiguity (unknown retrieval requirements).
Avoided committing full table contents as durable memory.

repair_options: If the system must support exact row-level lookups, store the raw rows as an external artifact (blob/file) with an index on id, and store only metadata plus summaries in the memory store.
If only aggregate or pattern-based queries are needed, store aggregated statistics and distinct value distributions instead of row-level data.
